*red apples*  
returns results with all terms like:

# Validation and analysis of the Polair3D v1.11 chemical transport model over Quebec

### Shoma Yamanouchi

### Shayamilla Mahagammulla Gamage

### Sara Torbatian

### Jad Zalzal

### Laura Minet

### Audrey Smargiassi

### Ying Liu

### Ling Liu

### Forood Azargoshasbi

### Jinwoong Kim

### Youngseob Kim

### Daniel Yazgi

### Marianne Hatzopoulou

Air pollution is a major health hazard, and while air quality overall has been improving in industrialized nations, pollution is still a major economic and public health issue, with some species, such as ozone (O3), still exceeding the standards set by governing agencies. Chemical transport models (CTMs) are valuable tools that aid in our understanding of the risks of air pollution both at local and regional scales. In this study, the Polair3D v1.11 CTM of the Polyphemus air quality modeling platform was set up over Quebec, Canada, to assess the model's capability in predicting key air pollutant species over the region, at seasonal temporal scales and at regional spatial scales. The simulation by the model included three nested domains, at horizontal resolutions of 9âkm by 9âkm and 3âkm by 3âkm, as well as two 1âkm by 1âkm domains covering the cities of MontrÃ©al and QuÃ©bec. We find that the model captures the spatial variability and seasonal effects and, to a lesser extent, the hour-by-hour or day-to-day temporal variability for a fixed location. The model at both the 3âkm and the 1âkm resolution struggled to capture high-frequency temporal variability and showed large variabilities in correlation and bias from site to site. When comparing the biases and correlation at a site-wide scale, the 3âkm domain showed slightly higher correlation for carbon monoxide (CO), nitrogen dioxide (NO2), and nitric oxide (NO), while ozone (O3), sulfur dioxide (SO2), and PM2.5 showed slight increases in correlation at the 1âkm domain. The performance of the Polair3D model was in line with other models over Canada and comparable to Polair3D's performance over Europe.

![LinkedIn](https://www.geoscientific-model-development.net/linkedin.png)

Yamanouchi, S., Gamage, S. M., Torbatian, S., Zalzal, J., Minet, L., Smargiassi, A., Liu, Y., Liu, L., Azargoshasbi, F., Kim, J., Kim, Y., Yazgi, D., and Hatzopoulou, M.: Validation and analysis of the Polair3D v1.11 chemical transport model over Quebec, Geosci. Model Dev., 17, 3579–3597, https://doi.org/10.5194/gmd-17-3579-2024, 2024.

Air pollution is a major health hazard that affects millions of lives globally and is seen as one of the largest contributors to global disability-adjusted life years ([GBD 2015 Risk Factors Collaborators](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx19),Â [2016](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx19)). While air quality overall has been improving in Canada, some species, such as ozone (O3), still regularly exceed the standards set by governing agencies (e.g.,Â [Ministry of the Environment and Climate Change](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx38),Â [2016](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx38)). Furthermore, the Canadian government ([Health Canada](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx21),Â [2022](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx21)[a](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx21)) estimated in 2019 that the economic impacts of air-quality-related health risks are over CADâ100 billion per year and that air pollution is linked to 15â300 premature deaths every year in Canada.

Industrial and traffic emissions play a large role in determining urban air quality (e.g.,Â [Rai](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx42),Â [2016](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx42); [Batisse etÂ al.](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx2),Â [2017](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx2); [Wallington etÂ al.](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx53),Â [2022](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx53); [Health Canada](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx22),Â [2022](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx22)[b](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx22)). In the province of Quebec in Eastern Canada, 410 premature deaths were attributed to traffic-related air pollution in 2015 ([Health Canada](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx22),Â [2022](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx22)[b](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx22)). Quebec sees higher levels of particulate matter than the national average and similar results for nitrogen dioxide (NO2) and sulfur dioxide (SO2). Additionally, industrial emissions and proximity to industrial facilities in Quebec have been associated with adverse health outcomes such as asthma onset in childhood ([Buteau etÂ al.](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx5),Â [2020](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx5)), short-term risk of hospitalization in children ([Brand etÂ al.](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx4),Â [2016](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx4)) and a decrease in lung function ([Smargiassi etÂ al.](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx48),Â [2014](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx48)). As opposed to traffic emissions which mainly take place in densely populated areas, high-emitting industries in Canada are also found in rural areas ([Jeong etÂ al.](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx26),Â [2011](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx26)). These regions typically do not have other major sources of air pollution, which results in large gradients in pollution levels in nearby communities.

Environment and Climate Change Canada (ECCC) operates about 250 air pollutant monitoring stations as part of their National Air Pollution Surveillance (NAPS) program ([NAPS](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx39),Â [2016](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx39)), of which 131 are in Quebec (and some may only be reporting limited time periods and/or limited pollutant species). Given the size of the country and the province, this is far too sparse to be useful in conducting spatial variability analyses of air pollutants. Modeling the sources, chemistry, dynamic transport of atmospheric pollutants is crucial in understanding tropospheric pollution events and mitigating health impacts by identifying affected regions and sensitivity to various emissions. There are mainly two chemical transport models (CTMs) used in Canada, GEM-MACH run by ECCC and the US EPA's Community Multiscale Air Quality Modeling System (CMAQ). While the ECCC GEM-MACH has been used to model the atmosphere over Canada including Quebec ([Chen etÂ al.](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx8),Â [2020](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx8); [Health Canada](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx22),Â [2022](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx22)[b](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx22)), we attempt to assess the performance of and validate the Polair3D CTM of the Polyphemus air quality modeling platform ([Mallet etÂ al.](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx35),Â [2007](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx35)) coupled with emissions derived from both ECCC and the United States emission inventories using the Sparse Matrix Operator Kernel Emissions (SMOKE) emissions-processing system, for key pollutant species: carbon monoxide (CO), O3, NO2, NO, SO2, and particulate matter smaller than 2.5âÂµm in diameter (PM2.5). The Polair3D model has seen little use over North America and particularly over Canada, aside from one example over Ontario, Canada ([Minet etÂ al.](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx37),Â [2021](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx37)), and a coarse-resolution study covering all of North America by [Sartelet etÂ al.](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx46) ([2012](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx46)). Unlike CMAQ, which is mainly used at larger, regional scales at coarser resolutions (i.e., horizontal resolutions higher than â¼1âkm2), Polair3D is known to be robust at these higher resolutions ([Thouron etÂ al.](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx50),Â [2017](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx50)), and we aim to assess the model performance at these higher resolutions as well as at coarser resolutions with larger modeling domains. In this study, we aim to present a novel use of this model over Quebec, Canada, using a longer modeling period and a larger modeling domain, to assess the ground (surface) level model performance at seasonal temporal scales and at regional spatial scales.

## 2.1âModel setup

The Polyphemus platform ([Mallet etÂ al.](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx35),Â [2007](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx35)) was used for this analysis. Polyphemus is an open-source suite of models developed at the Centre d'Enseignement et de Recherche en Environnement AtmosphÃ©rique (CEREA), and in this study, Polair3D ([Sartelet etÂ al.](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx45),Â [2002](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx45); [Mallet and Sportisse](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx34),Â [2004](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx34); [Pourchet etÂ al.](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx41),Â [2005](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx41); [Boutahar etÂ al.](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx3),Â [2004](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx3)), a CTM within the Polyphemus platform, was utilized. The newest version (v1.11) of the model ([Kim etÂ al.](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx28),Â [2023](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx28)) was used, with an updated aerosol chemistry module called SSH-aerosol ([Sartelet etÂ al.](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx44),Â [2020](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx44)). This module combines SCRAM (Size-Composition Resolved Aerosol Model), which simulates the dynamics and the mixing state of atmospheric particles; SOAP (Secondary Organic Aerosol Processor), which models the partitioning of organic compounds; and H2O (hydrophilic/hydrophobic organics), which simulates the formation of semi-volatile organic compounds formed via the oxidation of volatile organic compounds (VOCs). Polair3D is a Eulerian atmospheric CTM and includes preprocessing modules for formatting and creating binary input files for meteorology, biogenic emissions, surface deposition, and initial/boundary conditions. Anthropogenic emissions inventories are discussed in Sect.Â [2.2](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#Ch1.S2.SS2). For calculating biogenic emissions and deposition at the surface, land-use data from GLC2000 were used ([BartholomÃ© and Belward](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx1),Â [2005](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx1)).

The meteorology field was taken from pre-run WRF data ([NCAR](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx40),Â [2023](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx40)). The modeling domain comprises four domains that have 27, 9, 3, and 1âkm grid spacing, respectively, with two-way nesting. The number of vertical levels is 42, spanning from the surface to 100âhPa. Initial and lateral boundary conditions of meteorology were provided by the North American Regional Reanalysis (NARR) ([Mesinger etÂ al.](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx36),Â [2006](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx36)), which is available at a 32âkm grid spacing with 30 vertical levels. Each 30âh forecast was initialized every 00:00âUTC and had a 6âh spin-up time. Thus, the first 6âh of forecasts are discarded and replaced with forecasts initiated with the previous cycle for overlapping times. Grid nudging was applied for horizontal wind, temperature, and humidity for vertical levels above the planetary boundary layer (PBL) height in the largest domain. Parameterization schemes used in the simulation are as follows: the Purdue Lin scheme ([Chen and Sun](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx9),Â [2002](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx9)) for microphysics, Rapid Radiative Transfer Model for GCMs (RRTMG) shortwave and longwave schemes ([Iacono etÂ al.](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx24),Â [2008](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx24)), MellorâYamadaâJanjic PBL scheme ([JanjiÄ](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx25),Â [1994](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx25)), GrellâDevenyi ensemble scheme ([Grell and DÃ©vÃ©nyi](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx20),Â [2002](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx20)) for cumulus parameterization which was applied only to domain 1 and 2, Unified Noah land surface model ([Chen and Dudhia](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx6),Â [2001](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx6)), and a three-category urban canopy model ([Chen etÂ al.](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx7),Â [2011](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx7)) for urban areas. WRF temperature, as well as wind speed/direction, was compared against observational data from meteorological stations operated by ECCC prior to using them in Polair3D.

From this WRF configuration, meteorology from the 9, 3, and 1âkm domains was used. The model configuration is as follows: the model was run in three nested domains; the largest and coarsest-resolution domain was roughly 9âkm by 9âkm grid-cell resolution (edges), and within it, a smaller domain of about 3âkm by 3âkm resolution was run, and lastly, 1âkm by 1âkm resolution runs were performed over the cities of MontrÃ©al and QuÃ©bec. The modeling domains are shown in Fig.Â [1](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#Ch1.F1) (note that parts of the 9 and 3âkm domains include the United States (US)). The model was run for four seasons from 2018, with 4 weeks per season (January for winter, April for spring, July for summer, and October for fall), for a total of 16 weeks of model data. Spin-up was done for 1 week for each run. Boundary conditions for the outermost domain and the initial conditions for each of the runs were derived from CAM-Chem assimilated data ([Tilmes etÂ al.](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx51),Â [2015](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx51)).

The model was run with a 10âmin time step, and output was averaged and saved hourly. The model was run with vertical grids going up to 6000âm, but only data from the lowermost layer (surface) were saved. The vertical resolution is as follows: 0, 20, 40, 90, 150, 250, 400, 800, 1500, 2400, 3500, and 6000âm. For 1âkm resolution runs, the small domain size led to numerical instabilities, and the time steps were lowered to 5 and 1âmin for the cities of MontrÃ©al and QuÃ©bec, respectively. In this study, CO, O3, NO2, NO, SO2, and PM2.5 from the model were examined, although data for other species were also saved.

![https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/gmd-17-3579-2024-f01](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/gmd-17-3579-2024-f01-thumb.png)

**FigureÂ 1**The modeling domains used in this study.

## 2.2âEmissions

A Sparse Matrix Operator Kernel Emissions (SMOKE) emissions-processing system was used to prepare the Polair3D emissions input files ([CMAS-SMOKE](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx12),Â [2023](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx12)). Emission processing involves three major steps: spatial allocation, temporal allocation, and chemical speciation. Canadian and US emissions in the domain were calculated based on SMOKE-ready formats of the Canadian emission inventory ([Sassi etÂ al.](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx47),Â [2021](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx47)) and US National Emissions Inventory ([EPA: Emissions Modeling Platforms](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx18),Â [2023](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx18)), along with their temporal allocation and chemical speciation data. Spatial allocations for the three nested domains were generated using both Canadian and US spatial allocator inputs ([CMAS-SA](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx11),Â [2023](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx11); [CMAS-DB](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx10),Â [2023](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx10)).

Canada's Air Pollutant Emissions Inventory (APEI) also known as the Canadian criteria-air-contaminants (CACs) emissions inventory, is prepared and published by ECCC. The APEI is a comprehensive inventory of anthropogenic emissions of 17 air pollutants including CO, ammonia (NH3), nitrogen oxides (NO*x*), PM2.5, particulate matter smaller than 10âÂµm in diameter (PM10), SO2, and VOCs at the national, provincial, and territorial levels. It is compiled from many different data sources. The APEI is developed by the Pollutant Inventories and Reporting Division (PIRD) of ECCC. The inventory databases compiled by PIRD are modified by the Air Quality Modeling Applications Section (AQMAS) of ECCC for emissions processing with SMOKE. For further details of the SMOKE-ready format of the Canadian 2015 APEI inventory, refer to Annex 2 of the *1990â2015 Air Pollutant Emission Inventory Report* ([Environment and Canada](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx16),Â [2017](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx16)).

The US National Emissions Inventory (NEI) is the second inventory used in this study. NEI includes emissions for the six criteria air pollutants (CAPs) and 187 hazardous air pollutants. The CAP-related emissions are NH3, CO, Pb, NO*x*, particulate matter (PM2.5, PM10, organic carbon, and black carbon), SO2, and VOC. Data on US emissions are derived in several ways: continuous measurements, estimates based on infrequent source samples, and estimates based on average emission rates. In this study, we use a combination of SMOKE-ready formats of NEI 2014 and 2017 inventories from EPA ([EPA: Emissions Modeling Platforms](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx18),Â [2023](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx18)).

The emission sectors nonpoint, on road, and nonroad in both Canadian and US inventories were processed in SMOKE as area sources. The point source sectors in both inventories were processed as either two-dimension (2D) or three-dimension (3D) (layered) elevated point source emissions. Emissions from point sources such as airports and mines are considered 2D elevated sources. Industrial emissions (e.g., electric power generation, commercial facilities) are calculated as 3D layered emissions. SMOKE analyzes the stack parameters of each facility as well as the meteorology to determine the layers' emissions. SMOKE accesses the stack parameters such as the height, the diameter, and the temperature directly from the industrial emissions reported in the Canadian and US inventories. Meteorology-Chemistry Interface Processor (MCIP) was used to generate the necessary meteorology files (e.g., `GRID_CRO_2D`, `MET_CRO_2D`, `MET_DOT_3D`) for SMOKE volume emission processing ([EPA-CMAQ](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx17),Â [2023](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx17)). There are known aberrations in the ECCC off-road emissions in January. To correct for this, the off-road emissions in January were replaced by those of April (off-road emissions do not vary significantly by season). These aberrations were not seen in any of the other sectors nor in any of the other months.

`GRID_CRO_2D`
`MET_CRO_2D`
`MET_DOT_3D`

The Polair3D model contains a Size-Composition Resolved Aerosol Model (SCRAM). Thus, the PM AE6 speciated SMOKE output must be incorporated into an input for SCRAM. This conversion is shown in TableÂ [1](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#Ch1.T1). The size distribution of the PM species was applied based on the SNAP (Selected Nomenclature for Air Pollution) sectors. The SNAP sectors include combustion in energy and transformation industries; non-industrial combustion plant; combustion in the manufacturing industry; production processes; extraction and distribution of fossil fuels and geothermal energy; solvent and other products; road transport, other mobile sources, and machinery; waste treatment and disposal; and agriculture ([EMEP/EEA](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx14),Â [2019](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx14)). A 5-bin size distribution was applied to each species that was derived from the 10-bin SNAP size distribution values consistent with the Polair3D inputs. For the Polair3D model, primary organic aerosol (POA) species also need to be divided into three categories based on volatility. Hence, first, the POA species were divided into low (POAlP), medium (POAmP), and high (POAhP) volatility, and then we applied the 5-bin size distribution for each subspecies.

**TableÂ 1**SMOKE PM output conversion to Polair3D PM input.

![](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/gmd-17-3579-2024-t01-thumb.png)

[Download Print Version](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/gmd-17-3579-2024-t01.png)

## 2.3âSurface observations

Model results were compared against surface observations to validate the model and assess its performance in modeling key air pollutant species at the surface level. Surface observations collected as part of the National Air Pollution Surveillance (NAPS) program ([NAPS](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx39),Â [2016](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx39)) were used in this analysis.

The air pollution species examined in this study (CO, O3, NO2, NO, SO2, and PM2.5) are reported by some but not all NAPS sites; many NAPS sites only report some of the species, and some sites may not have data during the modeling time period.

To assess the modeling performance, several statistics were examined: Pearson correlation coefficient (*R*), mean relative difference (MRD), mean squared error (MSE), mean bias (MB), and normalized mean bias (NMB). MRD, MSE, NB, and NMB were calculated by subtracting NAPS from the model (i.e., MRD=100ÃModel-NAPSModel, MSE=E[(Model-NAPS)2], NB=Î£[Model-NAPS]N, and NMB=100ÃÎ£[Model-NAPS]Î£[NAPS] ). These statistics were chosen following [Emery etÂ al.](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx15) ([2017](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx15)).

Additionally, for NO2 and PM2.5, the model was compared against assimilated monthly ground level national LUR (land use regression) dataset products from the Canadian Urban Environmental Health Research Consortium (CANUE) (for NO2) ([Hystad etÂ al.](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx23),Â [2011](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx23); [Weichenthal etÂ al.](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx54),Â [2017](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx54); [DMTI Spatial Inc.](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx13),Â [2015](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx13)) and Atmospheric Composition Analysis Group (ACAG) for (PM2.5) ([van Donkelaar etÂ al.](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx52),Â [2021](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx52)). The CANUE NO2 dataset was developed from 2006 NAPS data using the land use regression model taking into account various geographic variables (road length within 10âkm, area of industrial land use within 2âkm, and summer rainfall) and satellite data (from 2005 to 2011) ([Hystad etÂ al.](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx23),Â [2011](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx23)). In this dataset, monthly averages of NO2 are given for each Canadian postal code ([DMTI Spatial Inc.](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx13),Â [2015](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx13)), and the comparison analysis with the model was done using the 3âkm resolution model binned into the closest model grid cell. The 2018 monthly dataset was used. The ACAG PM2.5 dataset is derived by assimilating aerosol optical depth (AOD) retrievals from the NASA MODIS, MISR, SeaWiFS, and VIIRS instruments with the GEOS-Chem chemical transport model and was calibrated to global ground-based observations using a geographically weighted regression ([van Donkelaar etÂ al.](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx52),Â [2021](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx52)). The dataset has a resolution of 0.01Â° by 0.01Â°. The comparison analysis was done using the 3âkm model resolution, and the ACAG dataset was binned into the Polair3D similar to the analysis done with the CANUE national LUR dataset. The 2018 monthly dataset was used.

## 2.4âTest scenarios

As discussed in Sect.Â [1](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#Ch1.S1), industrial emissions have been associated with adverse health outcomes. Understanding the behavior of the model under various emissions scenarios is important for performing pollution exposure analyses. To enrich the model validation findings and to qualitatively assess the behavior of the Polair3D model under varying emissions scenarios, a run with no industrial emissions was performed for the same domain and time frames. Other emissions (such as biogenic and traffic emissions) were kept the same. All other variables and input files, including the meteorology and the model configurations, were kept the same as the base case (i.e., with all emissions).

Additionally, two test scenarios, one with only the emissions from smelter, refinery, and foundry industries suppressed and another scenario with only the emissions from paper and pulp industry turned off, were run. These scenarios were adapted from a study by [Liu etÂ al.](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx31) ([2024](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/#bib1.bibx31)). As with the no-industry scenario, all other variables and input files were kept the same as the base case.

![https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/gmd-17-3579-2024-f02](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/17/3579/2024/gmd-17-3579-2024-f02-thumb.png)

All units are in Âµgâmâ3. Note that if the concentrations are above or below the scale (chosen here to exclude the lowest and the highest percentile), the figures will show white; this was done to preserve important spatial details in the mid-range of the data.

# Capturing Exposure Disparities with Chemical Transport Models: Evaluating the Suitability of Downscaling Using Land Use Regression

### Affiliations

# Capturing Exposure Disparities with Chemical Transport Models: Evaluating the Suitability of Downscaling Using Land Use Regression

### Authors

### Affiliations

## Abstract

High resolution exposure surfaces are essential to capture disparities in exposure to traffic-related air pollution in urban areas. In this study, we develop an approach to downscale Chemical Transport Model (CTM) simulations to a hyperlocal level (∼100m) in the Greater Toronto Area (GTA) under three scenarios where emissions from cars, trucks and buses are zeroed out, thus capturing the burden of each transportation mode. This proposed approach statistically fuses CTMs with Land-Use Regression using machine learning techniques. With this proposed downscaling approach, changes in air pollutant concentrations under different scenarios are appropriately captured by downscaling factors that are trained to reflect the spatial distribution of emission reductions. Our validation analysis shows that high-resolution models resulted in better performance than coarse models when compared with observations at reference stations. We used this downscaling approach to assess disparities in exposure to nitrogen dioxide (NO2) for populations composed of renters, low-income households, recent immigrants, and visible minorities. Individuals in all four categories were disproportionately exposed to the burden of cars, trucks, and buses. We conducted this analysis at spatial resolutions of 12, 4, 1 km, and 100 m and observed that disparities were significantly underestimated when using coarse spatial resolutions. This reinforces the need for high-spatial resolution exposure surfaces for environmental justice analyses.

**Keywords:**
air pollution; chemical transport models; downscaling; environmental justice; land use regression; machine learning; nitrogen dioxide; traffic emissions.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Chemical transport models (CTMs) simulate pollutant dispersion, interaction with atmospheric conditions, and chemical reactions, providing essential insights into air quality dynamics and health impacts.

The PALM model enhances urban air quality studies by integrating high-resolution meteorological data and pollutant sources, enabling precise predictions of air quality variations in urban environments.
CTMs are crucial for urban planning and policy-making, as they help assess air quality, identify pollution sources, and evaluate the effectiveness of mitigation strategies in densely populated areas.
Various case studies, including those in Quebec, illustrate CTM's ability to accurately predict pollutant levels, aiding in public health assessments and informing regulatory measures.
